rule: Dataclasses for simple data containers (not plain classes with only __init__)
verify: Classes that only store data use @dataclass or similar (Pydantic, NamedTuple).

rule: Comprehensions over loops when readable (ruff C401/C402)
verify: Simple transformations use comprehensions; complex logic uses loops.

rule: pathlib.Path over os.path (ruff PTH)
verify: No os.path usage where pathlib would work.

rule: Prefer composition over inheritance (>2 levels of inheritance is suspect)
verify: No deep inheritance hierarchies without strong justification.

rule: @property over get_/set_ methods (unless expensive operations)
verify: No get_x()/set_x() methods where @property would be appropriate.
exceptions: Operations that are expensive or have side effects (network calls, heavy computation) should use methods to signal cost

rule: pyproject.toml is the single source of truth (no setup.py/setup.cfg)
verify: No setup.py or setup.cfg exists in the project.

rule: Lock file for reproducible deploys (pip-compile or uv lock)
verify: A lock file exists and is committed to the repository.

rule: Ranges in pyproject.toml (>=1.2,<2.0), exact pins in lock file only
verify: pyproject.toml uses version ranges, not exact pins.

rule: Editable installs for development: pip install -e ".[dev]"
verify: Development setup uses editable install.

rule: Minimal dependencies — each must be imported somewhere in src/
verify: Every declared dependency is actually imported in the source code.

rule: Document why each dependency is needed (inline comments in pyproject.toml)
verify: Each dependency in pyproject.toml has a comment explaining its purpose.

rule: Consistent format: Google style or NumPy style (not both in one project)
verify: All docstrings use the same format.

rule: Include Args, Returns, Raises sections where applicable (ruff D417)
verify: Functions with parameters document all args; functions that raise document exceptions.

rule: Module structure: docstring -> imports -> constants -> classes -> functions -> if __name__
verify: Out-of-order elements are violations.

rule: One class per file for large classes (>200 lines). Small related classes can share.
verify: No file contains multiple large unrelated classes.

rule: Generators for large datasets iterated once (yield over return list)
verify: Functions producing large sequences use yield unless the caller needs random access.

rule: __slots__ for classes with many instances
verify: High-instance-count classes define __slots__.

rule: @lru_cache/@cache for pure functions called repeatedly with same args
verify: Repeatedly-called pure functions with same inputs use caching.

rule: Testing: pytest + pytest-cov (--cov-branch --cov-fail-under=100) + pytest-asyncio. Exclusions: # pragma: no cover — <reason>
verify: pytest --cov=<pkg> --cov-branch --cov-fail-under=100 passes. Every pragma has a reason.

rule: Configuration: lightshield/configs_loader_python for all config needs
verify: No hand-rolled argparse or config file parsing. Use ConfigsLoader with Field() declarations.

rule: Logging: lightshield/logger_python
verify: No print() for runtime logging. All logging through lightshield logger.

rule: Prefix private members with underscore (_private_member)
verify: Non-public members used only internally are underscore-prefixed.

rule: File naming: test_<module>.py
verify: All test files follow the naming convention.

rule: Fixtures for setup (no setUp/tearDown)
verify: No setUp/tearDown methods — use @pytest.fixture instead.

rule: @pytest.mark.parametrize for repetitive test patterns
verify: Repetitive test cases with same logic but different inputs use parametrize.

rule: Every test has a type marker: @pytest.mark.e2e / .integration / .unit / .learning. E2E tests also require @pytest.mark.req('REQ-XX.X').
verify: pytest --strict-markers passes. No test exists without a type marker. All e2e tests have a req marker. Unit/integration tests may optionally have req markers for navigation.

rule: Architecture enforcement: import-linter or pytest-archon to define and verify component boundaries.
verify: lint-imports passes in CI. Boundary contracts are defined in config (layers, independence, forbidden imports). New cross-package imports without a corresponding integration test are flagged.

rule: Prefer early exit (guard clauses) to reduce nesting
verify: For each nested if/else, ask: could this be an early return? tool_assist narrows candidates but does not verify: PLR5501 only flags collapsible-if (if inside if with no else) — it misses deeply nested code that could use guard clauses, and cannot judge whether refactoring is appropriate for the specific logic.

bad.why: Unnecessary nesting obscures the main logic

rule: No magic strings — extract to named constants for identity/matching values
verify: Strings used as identity values, matching patterns, keys, or repeated in multiple locations need named constants even if used once.

bad.why: Duplication caused a real bug when one was typo'd
String identity check — should be a constant

rule: No WHAT comments — extract to functions with meaningful names instead. WHY comments are acceptable when non-obvious, but rare.
verify: Every inline comment answers 'why', not restates the code. Structured docstrings (Google/NumPy style with Args/Returns/Raises) are API documentation, not comments — they are REQUIRED by document-public-api and are never violations of this rule. Section-heading comments in long functions for navigation (# --- Phase 1 ---) are also acceptable.

bad.why: Comment restates what the code already says

good.code:
# Retry needed because upstream API returns stale cache for ~2s after write
retry_with_backoff(fetch_latest)

exceptions: Structured docstrings (Args, Returns, Raises sections) — these are API docs, not comments
Section-heading comments in long functions for visual navigation

rule: Fail fast — validate inputs early
verify: Validation happens before main logic begins, not after side effects.

rule: Function names describe WHAT, not HOW
verify: Read call sites — does the name make calling code read like a story? Generic verbs (process, handle, resolve) without a specific object are suspect.

bad.why: Generic verbs that tell you nothing about the domain

rule: Functions stay at one abstraction level
verify: Are all statements at the same level of detail?

bad.code: Mixing 'decide what to review' with 'construct SSH command string'
bad.why: Strategic decision and low-level string building in same function

good.code: Top-level reads as a story, details one level down in helpers

rule: Name things by intent, not implementation
verify: Would this name still make sense if the implementation changed?
exceptions: Multiple implementations where the name distinguishes them (fetch_diff_gerrit vs fetch_diff_local)

rule: Wrap third-party libraries behind your own interface. Never scatter direct calls throughout the codebase.
verify: Third-party imports appear only in wrapper/adapter modules, not in business logic. Swapping the lib means changing one file.

rule: README opens with WHY: what problem it solves, what it is, and what differentiates it — in that order, before any instructions.
verify: First paragraph answers: what problem? what is this? why this over alternatives? If it jumps straight to 'how to install', it's missing context.

rule: Single README.md at root — project description, quick start, links to docs
verify: No duplicate READMEs in subdirectories (unless independent packages).

rule: Split docs by audience: user/ (how to use) and developer/ (how it works)
verify: No user-facing content in developer/ and vice versa.

rule: Documentation assets live with documentation (not in src/)
verify: All markdown references resolve to existing files.

rule: All public API surfaces have structured documentation comments that tools can extract into generated docs.
verify: Every public function/class/module has a doc comment. Parameters, return values, and errors are documented. A doc generator produces usable output.

rule: Every requirement must have derived acceptance criteria — each with one observable outcome and a concrete verify example. No 'and' in an AC.
verify: Each requirement has one or more ACs inline. Each AC has: (1) unique ID, (2) one observable outcome, (3) concrete verify with input → expected output. ACs never contain 'and' (split instead). RTM operates at AC level — requirement is covered when ALL its ACs have passing tests. If the file grows large, generate views (flat checklist, uncovered-only).

bad.code: REQ-04.1: Entry agent is always the orchestrator
bad.why: No acceptance criteria. No concrete verify. A test can check internal ordering without verifying the user-visible result.

good.code:
REQ-04.1: Entry agent is always the orchestrator
  AC-04.1.1: Entry block executes first
    verify: Run team with 3 blocks → execution log shows entry block at position 1
  AC-04.1.2: Each block creates a task record
    verify: Run 'guild team' with 2-block team → 'guild history' shows 2 sub-tasks
  AC-04.1.3: Team execution logged in audit
    verify: Run 'guild team' → 'guild audit' contains 'task_created' entries

rule: Levels: TRACE (flow) -> DEBUG (state) -> INFO (business events) -> WARNING (handled unexpected) -> ERROR (needs attention)
verify: Each log statement uses the correct level. Heuristic: does this require human action? Yes → ERROR. Unexpected but handled, no action needed → WARNING. Internal state for debugging → DEBUG. TRACE is for raw payloads and loop iterations.

bad.why: Cache misses are normal operation, not errors — should be DEBUG
Connection failure needs attention — should be WARNING or ERROR

good.code: logger.debug('Cache miss for key %s, fetching from source', key)
logger.error('DB connection failed after 3 retries: %s', exc)

rule: INFO tells the story — reading only INFO logs explains what the system did
verify: Reading only INFO-level output gives a coherent narrative of system behavior.

rule: Log content: always include context (what failed, what input, what expected). Never log secrets.
verify: No bare messages without context variables. No secrets near log calls.

bad.why: No context about what failed or why

good.code: logger.error('Failed to process task %s: %s', task_id, exc)

rule: Group by domain, not by file type or grab-bag. No utils/, helpers/, misc/, models/, views/, controllers/.
verify: Every directory name describes a domain or capability. No type-grouped or generic-bucket directories.

rule: Root level: only entry points and config files. Tech convention files (pyproject.toml, package.json, .gitignore, Makefile) are acceptable.
verify: No implementation source files at root.
exceptions: Trivial single-file projects (scripts, small utilities) with no directory structure may have implementation at root.

rule: Max 3-4 directory levels
verify: Deeper nesting signals the component should be its own package.

rule: No orphaned or build artifact files in git
verify: git ls-files has no node_modules/, dist/, __pycache__/.

rule: Separate concerns: src/, tests/ (mirrors src), docs/, scripts/
verify: Test directory structure mirrors source directory structure.

rule: Configuration centralized in one module — no scattered hardcoded values
verify: Grep for config values — they originate from one place.

rule: Each function/class does one thing (Single Responsibility — SOLID S)
verify: Can you name its purpose without a conjunction?

rule: Open for extension, closed for modification (SOLID O)
verify: Can new behavior be added without changing existing code? If adding a feature requires modifying a switch statement or if/else chain, the design violates this.
exceptions: Simple scripts and one-off tools where extensibility isn't needed.

rule: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base type (SOLID L)
verify: Can every subclass be used wherever the base class is expected without surprises? Methods that throw 'not implemented' or silently no-op are violations.

rule: No client should depend on methods it doesn't use (SOLID I)
verify: Does any class implement an interface where it stubs out or ignores some methods? If yes, the interface should be split.

rule: Depend on abstractions, not concretions (SOLID D)
verify: High-level modules import abstract interfaces, not concrete implementations. Concrete types are injected at construction time.

rule: Each acceptance criterion maps to at least one assertion in an E2E test. A requirement is covered only when ALL its ACs have passing assertions.
verify: Count ACs for the requirement. Count assertions in the linked E2E test. If assertions < ACs, there is a gap. Automated check: parse ACs from REQUIREMENTS.md, parse test assertions tagged with the AC ID, report any AC without a corresponding assertion.

bad.why: Tests implementation details. Breaks if implementation changes. Tells you nothing about whether the requirement is met.

good.code:
# Happy path: frequency is removed
original = generate_sine_wave(freq=1000)
filtered = system.apply_notch(original, freq_khz=1.0)
assert magnitude_at(filtered, 1000) < magnitude_at(original, 1000) * 0.1

# Sad path: invalid frequency
with pytest.raises(ValueError):
    system.apply_notch(original, freq_khz=-1.0)

# Edge case: frequency at Nyquist limit
result = system.apply_notch(original, freq_khz=22.0)
assert result is not None

rule: Every component boundary must have at least one integration test where both sides are real (not mocked).
verify: A boundary registry lists all component connections (A→B), derived automatically from code structure (e.g., cross-package import analysis). Each connection has a traceable integration test. CI automation detects new boundaries and flags untested ones.

bad.code:
runner = TeamRunner(storage=MockStorage())
store = Storage(':memory:')
# Both pass independently. No test runs them together.
bad.why: Absent wiring is invisible to branch coverage. Only an integration test forces the connection to exist.

good.code:
store = Storage(tmp_path / 'test.db')
await store.connect()
runner = TeamRunner(team=team_def, storage=store)
await runner.run('task')
assert len(await store.list_tasks()) >= 2

exceptions: External systems (LLM, cloud) may use contract tests or recorded responses
UI→API boundaries may use E2E browser tests instead

rule: Tests are independent and repeatable
verify: Random order execution produces same results.

rule: Tests must be deterministic. A test that sometimes passes and sometimes fails is worse than no test.
verify: Running the full suite 3 times in a row produces identical results. No tests rely on wall-clock time, network availability, random values without seeding, or race conditions. Flaky tests are either fixed immediately or deleted — they are never skipped or retried into passing.

rule: Each test verifies one logical behavior
verify: Multiple asserts on same result are fine; unrelated behaviors in one test are not.

rule: Test names describe behavior
verify: Names read as sentences (test_returns_empty_when_no_matches, not test_func1). tool_assist narrows candidates but does not verify: grep flags test names lacking behavioral verbs (returns, raises, creates, fails, etc.) but cannot judge whether a name is truly descriptive of the behavior being tested.
tool_output: [grep:test functions without behavioral verbs (returns|raises|creates|fails|when|should|with)]: manual grep required by reviewer

rule: Write learning tests for third-party libraries to verify your assumptions about their behavior.
verify: Each third-party dependency has at least one test exercising the behavior you rely on. Breaks on upgrade if API changed.

rule: No magic numbers — extract to named constants
verify: For each flagged numeric literal, determine if it's truly magic or a standard value in Python context.

bad.why: What is special about 3? Intent is invisible.

exceptions: Loop bounds derived from container size
Math constants used in context (/ 2 for midpoint)
Default parameter values that are self-evident
tool_output:
PLR2004 Magic value used in comparison, consider replacing `2` with a constant variable
   --> src/guild/artifacts/manager.py:115:30
    |
113 |         for p in sorted(task_dir.iterdir()):
114 |             parts = p.name.rsplit(".v", 1)
115 |             if len(parts) != 2 or not parts[1].isdigit():
    |                              ^
116 |                 continue
117 |             name, ver = parts[0], int(parts[1])
    |

PLR2004 Magic value used in comparison, consider replacing `3` with

rule: No nested ternary operators
verify: No nested inline if/else expressions. Single-level ternary is acceptable for simple value selection.

bad.code: result = a if x else (b if y else c)

rule: Functions: ~50 lines max
verify: Flag any function exceeding 50 lines. tool_assist narrows candidates but does not verify: PLR0915 counts statements, not lines — a function with 40 statements across 60 lines (due to multi-line expressions) would be missed, and a function with 55 single-line statements would be flagged even at exactly 55 lines. LLM must judge actual line count and whether the function's length is justified.

rule: Parameters: 5 max
verify: Flag functions with >5 parameters. tool_assist narrows candidates but does not verify: PLR0913 flags functions exceeding the configured parameter limit, but LLM must judge whether the parameters are justified (e.g., constructor injection of dependencies, or a well-known API signature).

   |
